speaker: interviewer
text: Earlier, you were telling us about the family gathering and talking about what was going on in pre-war Europe. Can you come back and describe that a little bit more and tell us about your recollections?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The, the frightening thing about being a young person, a young child, at my age, which was six, six, seven years old-- no, it was six years old, is that, you know, traditionally, you come home, here, under normal conditions. And you kind of shield the bad things from children. You say let's not talk in front of a child about something bad that's going to happen. Let's be more positive and, and just expose them to the better, to the nice, niceties, niceties. But unfortunately, everything in our existence in those days was so dismal and so morbid that there was just no good news. There was just nothing good that was, was, was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the elders-- since the community was so small, and everything was congested that you couldn't but hear these terrible tales and these terrible stories that were coming back and, and that the elders heard on radio and that-- what was happening around us in Europe and some of the countries that were already captured and occupied by the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were not yet aware of concentration camps and that sort of thing, but the impression to a young child was already nothing, but you-- you-- you-- you-- you envisioned, your imagination was death. Death and death and death and death. So in one way, you were being prepared for what was happening, rather than just a, a, a sudden thing happening to you. But, but it was a-- it was a scary and horrible, horrible way of growing up. And each day-- each day it got a little worse. And each day you started hearing the bombings, and then ultimately, these planes were just flying over on a round-the-clock basis, and just bombing and bombing and bombing and bombing. While the Polish army was still fighting, which wasn't very long, but for the period of four or five weeks, we were subjected to a constant-- you know, the, the hissing of that bomb falling. And you get-- you never get used to that. And you're scared to death of it. And as a child, that's another impression that you heard that whistling of a bomb, and you don't-- you never know when and how close it's going to get to you. And I was living on the day-- drawn shades. And lights had to be off during the day. And you were permitted only to use lights at night. Lights-- lights were comprised of maybe two or three bulbs in the-- in the apartment. And you had to draw your shades, and there better not be any cracks or any, any visibility throughout. And you were just kind of growing in that environment, and that environment was a scary, scary, terrible environment for a young child to be in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in a way, I was being prepared, in my own little mind, to cope with it, because your mother and your father couldn't continue to say to you well, don't worry, don't worry, because there was something to worry. And, and how far can you protect and shield? But as you grow up, you kind of form your own scaredness and your own way of existing and your own way of coping with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Playing with your friends becomes secondary. And now you're starting to be a little more hungry, and there's a little less food available. And the things that you had prior is now not so available. And you're now in that mode and that-- on the road of existing rather than living. And things don't get better. They just get worse, progressively worse. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] now we don't have food, now we rationed, this ghetto. And we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we-- can we stay a little bit longer before the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, when you say before, you mean during the-- during the part that-- the invasion part?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, do you remember when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in-- the war broke out, and we-- I was, I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought I'd hear about it before the Germans started invading Poland, because we weren't isolated. We were-- we were able to hear-- When I say we, I hear-- as a child, you always heard from your elders, during a conversation, during the, the, the time when adults are talking-- what are we going to do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Some were talking about should we leave? It was too late to leave. Some had the opportunity to leave before to the United-- to America, to wherever. And some did go, like, prior to the invasion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then a lot of the adults were saying we should have, we could have-- and, and you just hear these things, and you just grow into these things. And as a child, you're kind of helpless. I mean, you, you really have no choice. You're there, and you wouldn't go anywhere, anyway, because this is your family, your mother, your father, and so on. But there was-- there was a preparation for, I would say, today, I know what the time element was. Then, it'll seem forever. But I would say it was-- before the actual [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] takeover, I would say there was about three months of preparations, living under these conditions. Well, the Polish government told us to draw the shades and, and, and be careful about exposing ourselves to the bombings and to the-- to the planes that were continually flying over our cities and bombing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got a pretty good lesson in that endeavor. So growing up under these conditions was a-- was a daily chore to each of us-- I mean, from the youngest to the ones that could walk and, and understand, like I was beginning to, to the zaydes, to the grown people. And you just grow into it. And that's all I can say on, on, on, on pre-- pre-invasion or pre-occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I was not in the army, since I was not privy to any of the-- what an adult could and would participate in, I was just a young bystander, as a child, but I formed my own-- my own way of coping with it. Any child, I think, does-- here, any place else. You sort of form your own, and you do your own little way, and you cope with it. You sort of-- it's like a young boy getting another brother or sister, or a sister getting a brother. Somehow, they just cope with it. It's, it's not a good thing, and nothing that the parents or anyone can say to them. They have to sort of work it with them-- themselves. And, and this is what happens under bad and terrible conditions, not just for me, but for any young child under any extenuating circumstances. They form their own life, their, their way of coping with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the things that you did to cope with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was scared all the time. I was just-- was just, just scared. I mean, you hear these things flying and bombing. And every time that happens, your mother grabs you, and she runs into that room. And under the window, that was your best place, is right under the window. Because that way if anything collapsed, it collapses in the middle of the room rather than at that end. Then you were worried about glass. And it was-- it was, you heard those alarms go off, and it was just like panic. And that's how you grew up, and so that's what I recollect. It was like never, ever having a peace of mind. That, that child experience that I had pre-bombings, which was running around and getting in trouble and playing football, and so on, that became a part of a past that was no longer. It was just a matter of now how not to be bombed, I suppose, or, or, or just be safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was just terrible, terrible, terrible daily existence. But the good thing about it was that we were still all together. And we held onto each other. So that's what I remember as a child. Was your father traveling at this time, or was he home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The traveling was over. There was-- there was nothing there to travel. There was nothing to play. There was no place to play. And it was the beginning of the end of survival, of, of of becoming a different-- a different existence, totally different [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] human being, another world. That world was just fast and faster disappearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when the Nazis occupied Lodz, the day they came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- when you say Lodz, you know, I-- you know, it was done systematically. I do remember when they started to be more visible. And my parents would prepare me for that experience, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was constantly kept inside. I was not to go out. So I was, like, inside forever, and we started living inside. And any playing we did as children, we did inside. There was no, no outside anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the stories, then, were changing, you know, from Hitler invading to what we're going to do and how we should have done it. Now, it's, it's here, and, and now what do we do? And what's going to happen tomorrow? It was no longer next week or what should we have done a year ago? Now we're confronted with the devil himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and no matter how I imagine how badly they-- anyone perceived what was going to happen, it turned out infinitely greater, so that we were instantly cut off from everything, and the instant brutality, the instant orders, the instant change was not a, a slower way of introducing you into that kind of existence. It was like the door shutting on you, and that's it. And the separation from the Gentile area to the Jewish quarters was almost instantaneous. The separation of building, building fences and building-- virtually building fences up and down streets in the ghetto to separate you from the Gentile versus the Jewish quarters, the building of bridges where you had to cross streets. The-- we were-- the-- we, we would-- had to build these humongous bridges to cross over to-- the ghetto was, like, comprised of-- if this was a main street, they would build-- they would build fences on both sides of the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the middle would be for the military or for streetcars or transportation. And on each side, the Jews resided. So in order for you to go to see your Aunt Zelda, for the other side, you either had to go to some point of that street or some distance to get into a gate to either cross it or to a, a shorter way where they would make-- where they built bridges, where you virtually had to cross over a bridge to go on the other side. As a--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the order was that children-- which I was one of those-- qualified, we had to go to work. And I worked in a furniture-- wood finishing place. I was, at that time, seven, seven years old. And we were required to have identification cards to-- that we had to go to work. And I worked in this Tischler Resort. A Tischler Resort is where you build-- make furniture. And my job was to just polish, all day long, the wood. They would give us this chemical, and we'd just put this chemical on, on, on unfinished wood. And that was my job. But it was-- methodically, we were just being shrunk into a-- into a-- in a sort of a cubicle, where our freedoms, our lives, our existence, our anything, was being denied us on a daily basis. So again, I go back to the point that the wonderful thing, still, about the ghetto-- you know this thing has so many tentacles-- that it was wonderful at that particular time because of this, and the next time it was-- got worse, but it was still good because of that. And the third time that was taken away from you, it was still wonderful, because you were still able to play and do this a little bit or that. Now, it was still wonderful, under all these terrible conditions and under all these-- situation-- under the situation. I was still with my mother, and we still were a family unit. And to a child, that is the most wonderful, protective, warm, indescribable feeling-- is to be with your parents under any conditions, virtually any conditions. I don't care if you have to share a rat's tail to-- for food. It is the love of your mother who hugs you and the nice words and so on. So in a way, under even those microscopic conditions, it was still a good condition, because I was still in my family. We were still a unit. And as the war progressed, and as we continued in the ghetto, the Germans virtually built a whole government, a government of, of, of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like you read, it was the Master Plan. They, they enlisted and incorporated our own people. They built a police force. They built two police forces, some of a higher order, some of a lower order, that I lived with, that I saw, that I was part of. And the scare tactics now began from the inside. And you were rewarded if you did something for the Germans from the inside. So if [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you wanted to inform on somebody you didn't like, then you went to the [GERMAN], or you went to a Jewish policeman, or you went to someone that has contact with the Germans. And you said Sam Kellman has got a lot of gold, which I know he has been stashing for the last 10, 15, 20 years. And that was the whole crux for the Germans. They wanted to extract all the, the values-- the valuables from the people, I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I lived this. They came next door, they came down the street, they came to my cousins, they came to my friends' parents. I lived that. It isn't something I read about. That isn't something that someone told me, [GERMAN]. I know it, and I've lived it, OK? So-- And that was the conversation in the house always. [INAUDIBLE], they beat him, and they took him, and they did this. And they took him down to Gestapo headquarters. And the next time you saw [INAUDIBLE], he was like a vegetable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it isn't something that, that, that you have-- that, you know, see on television, which of course, was nonexistent at the time. But it's something that you lived on a daily basis. Since you-- we all lived as a very close community, you had the opportunity to see it and observe it. You saw [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] specific people who were taken by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. I saw them being taken, uncles of mine, and, and friends' parents, and they would come back a few days later with, with, with just-- or some never came back. And-- but those that came back were beaten to a-- to a-- to a pulp. And, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a method to-- it was a method to the madness. Because the thing is give it up, you shouldn't have it, and you're better off giving it now without being-- without this happening to you. So it was always a lesson.

speaker: interviewer
text: The great lesson in the Lodz ghetto was that each time someone committed something like that or against something that was the rule, it was to bring everybody out on a marktplatz. The marktplatz was the center of a city, like if-- how can I say it-- if you live in Shaker, you'd say, everybody has to come to Shaker Square and observe a hanging or a-- some sort of a punishment, because of something that happened, OK? And since the area was comparatively small, it wasn't very far to walk to view, having to go there. So you didn't have-- you know when you talk of a ghetto, you talk of maybe the size of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] perhaps not knowing the geographical distances, but I would say it couldn't be bigger-- the whole ghetto couldn't be bigger than half of Solen. Or-- it was that congested and that compact. For people who don't know how big Solen is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would say it would be three miles, OK? So it isn't very far to walk three miles, if you're told-- you're made to be-- walk. Now, I may be wrong in the, in the numbers, but it always seemed to me that we walked everywhere to, to be-- to be able to, to, to get to the places to-- that we were required and were forced to. So not specifically knowing distance, and saying that I know-- know that it was just-- This place was a place where everything was, was to be displayed by the Germans. It's that if you committed something against the, the, the rules and the order of, of things, then you were either punished. Or when there were hangings, everyone had to view those hangings. So I was a participant-- I mean, I was a viewer in, in-- to those hangings. So it wasn't something that I was not privy to. So yes, I, I was very much part of the, the process of viewing everything that was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a particular incident in the marktplatz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was a time where someone was trying to escape. And there was a group of guys, they were-- they were calling themselves the, the, the underground. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some of them would call themselves for the Haganah. Some would call themselves for the-- after the Russians, the Russian underground. I don't know, it was to be a, a, a term that the young-- the younger guys would align with-- is, is that there were-- they wanted to fight, and so on. And they caught these guys. There was a group of them, and took them right out. There was about six or seven of them. They hung them right on the, on the marktplatz. And this German, who was the head of the ghetto, his name was Biebow, supposedly a relative to Himmler and one of the higher ranking people in the, in the, in the, in the Reich. And he was in charge of the ghetto, and he was-- he was quite a shrewd individual and ran it like a-- like a precision instrument. And had two personalities, obviously. He was able to retain or maintain order and somehow prevent a what we would call an uprising, as, as, as had happened in Warsaw. And he had a way about him, where he would delegate-- like we had the oldest Jew, which was Chaim Rumkowski. He made him represent all the people, and they had hospitals, and they had their own police, and they had their own kitchens. And he says here, you run yourself, and so on. But when the knife had to be used, he was the-- it was precision, this guy. And when it was time to convince us that we're not leaving to concentration camps to be slaughtered-- as the rumors were then becoming very, very, very vivid and very clear-- he told the women, and he told the professional men, and he told everybody, on the marktplatz, that we're going-- this is as the Russians were beginning to invade the fronts of the other side of Poland-- that they wanted us to-- that they're taking us to Germany, and they're going to-- they have wonderful places for us to work in. And we're going to work for them. And it's going to be wonderful conditions. And that the women should bring their pots and sewing utensils and all the things that you need to run a household, and the-- because these things were very scarce in Germany, due to the war. And they told musicians to bring their instruments, the [NON-ENGLISH], the shoemakers, to bring their tools, and everyone should pack and bring everything they can, because these things are not available and so they have them to go to work. And he described a perfect utopia for where we were going to go. And he apparently got away with it, though he at least-- I suppose under certain conditions, you'll listen to any story, you know. And the elders listened to the story. And maybe the people in Warsaw didn't want to listen to that story because they were already-- their minds were made up that this story isn't going to fly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, I suppose, where there's hope, where there is some sort of an inkling of some hope, you will listen to anything. And so consequently, everybody started packing, and, and whether you believed them or not, you wanted to believe it. And that's how the, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] movement started to, to, to, to extract us out of the ghetto into the, into the, into the wagons on our way to the concentration camps. So-- this was a process-- this was a process of--- you know, this was a process that took almost four years. I'd like to go back, still earlier, and talk a little bit more about some of the earlier things. Did you have to move into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we already lived-- the ghetto in, in, in Lodz was a Jewish-- there was a Jewish quarters, and some Jews lived outside of those quarters. When I say quarters, I mean, it's like in-- possibly in the olden days. And, and as you came into this country, you had the Irish communities that sort of gathered there, and the Yiddish, and the Italians, and Greeks, and so on, and so forth. And you're comfortable. And to this day, we still have those communities, and so there was the Jewish quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- in, in, in Lodz, in that particular time, where we lived was the Jewish quarters. So this is how they took it geographically, saying well, since we have the majority of the Jews in this part of the area anyway, this is where we're going to form the ghetto. And those that were outside that perimeter would then be transported into the ghetto. And then-- and then wire off the ghetto and enclose the ghetto, so to speak, which they did with the-- with the fences and the bridges. So there's-- the answer to your question is there was already-- if you want to call it-- a ghetto, or a community, a Jewish community, except that they just elaborated on the-- on bringing everyone else in and then closing it up. I meant to ask you before to describe your apartment to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: The apartment was a, a-- I think, it was, like, seven stories, or six, seven stories high. I do remember we lived on the third floor. I always ran up. And as a kid, we always had races trying to go up. There was no elevator, so we-- everything-- and for the elders, too. So you were out of luck if you were on the sixth, seventh floor, because it was pretty steep and-- It was sort of dark. You know, there was-- electricity, in-- at the best of times, even before the war, was a luxury in, in Poland. So everything was not, you know-- everything was in terms of, of, of, of an experience, an adventure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the apartment, itself, the suite in which you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: The suite comprised of a, a bedroom, a living room, a little what I would call, like, a studio, which the-- our piano was. And the kitchen was in the hallway, like it was away from an apartment. So the kitchen, as we knew-- the kitchen was a stove sometimes that you cooked under coal or under wood, and then your pots were on top of that. And that's how we cooked in those days. So there was no exhaust. So the aroma would come for a block down the street. And it was a good aroma, so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The apartment, it was, like, maybe three rooms, which was considered a, a fairly good, good, good living quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we need to stop now to change the tape again.
